front: Mass: Apocryphally convinced the Council of Trent not to ban polyphony from worship
Mass: All movements except the Agnus Dei are scored for six voices; second Agnus Dei adds a seventh voice with an ascending fourth head-motif
Mass: A missa sine nomine (mass without a name); a version was arranged for orchestra by J. S. Bach
Opera: In this 1917 work, the title character initially refuses a commission from Cardinal Borromeo, who then imprisons him
Composer: His style was codified as the model for Renaissance counterpoint in Johann Joseph Fux's Gradus ad Parnassum
Composer: In Fux's Gradus ad Parnassum, the character Aloysius represents this composer; the student Josephus represents Fux
Composer: Key style principle — leaps are countered by stepwise movement in the opposite direction; dissonances fall on weak beats only
Composer: The term 'species counterpoint' was introduced in reference to attempts to imitate this composer's style
Motet: A cycle of 29 motets setting texts from the Song of Solomon (Canticum canticorum)
Composer: Composed over 250 motets and 140 madrigals; said he 'blushed and grieved' over his secular madrigals
Motet: Six-part motet featuring unisons, word painting, and a strong plagal cadence; the first polyphonic setting of this text
Motet: Emphasizes the word 'dominum' through homorhythmic repetition before a triple-time Alleluja section
Mass: Palestrina wrote two of these — one for five voices and one for four voices — based on the secular cantus firmus melody
Motet: An eight-part setting for unaccompanied double chorus; possibly written for Pope Gregory XIV
Motet: Based on Psalm 42; compares the soul to a deer thirsting for water; features long melismas on the word 'desiderat'
Composer: Appointed by Pope Julius III to direct the Cappella Giulia; later dismissed from the papal choir for being married
Composer: Resigned from St. John Lateran — for which he had written Lamentations — after a dispute over feeding his choirboys
Composer: Credited with producing the new 'gradual' promoted by the Cecilian Movement; the Sanctus of Anton Bruckner's Mass in E Minor quotes a theme by this composer
Composer: First publication modeled on that of Cristobal de Morales; joined with Annibale Zoilo in a failed attempt to restore the plainsong
Mass: The Kyrie introduces the recurring motif D–D, D–G–F–E–D
Mass: Named for a pope (Marcellus II) who reigned for only three weeks
Mass: The Tallis Scholars paired this Palestrina work with Allegri's Miserere in their debut recording
Mass: This missa sine nomine may have influenced Bach's Mass in B Minor
